steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence reads: 'a single tree like this, claims ______ Valerie Trouet, can tell the history of the world.' The word in the blank identifies Valerie Trouet's profession. When a noun directly renames or identifies another noun (an appositive), no punctuation separates them.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A: dendrochronologist—: A dash here would suggest that what follows is an interruption or elaboration, but 'Valerie Trouet' is simply the person making the claim, not an elaboration. The dash incorrectly disrupts the flow. ✗
  • Choice B — dendrochronologist,: A comma between the appositive noun 'dendrochronologist' and the name it renames, 'Valerie Trouet,' incorrectly separates the two noun elements of the subject phrase. Appositives directly preceding the noun they rename take no punctuation between them. ✗
  • Choice C — dendrochronologist: No punctuation between 'dendrochronologist' and 'Valerie Trouet' is correct. The appositive noun 'dendrochronologist' immediately renames Valerie Trouet with no separator needed. ✓
  • Choice D — dendrochronologist:: A colon introduces a list or explanation that follows, but 'Valerie Trouet' is a name, not a list or explanation. The colon is grammatically incorrect here. ✗
That matches Choice C — dendrochronologist.
